Learning Standards

big_ideas_elaborations.story/stories: narrative texts, whether real or imagined, that teach us about human nature, motivation, and experience, and often reflect a personal journey or strengthen a sense of identity. They may also be considered the embodiment of collective wisdom. Stories can be oral, written, or visual, and used to instruct, inspire, and entertain listeners and readers.
big_ideas_elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —

curricular_competencies.text: Use sources of information and prior knowledge to make meaning

elaborations.reading, listening, and viewing strategi: examples include distinguishing drawing from writing, asking questions to construct and clarify meaning, using active listening, predicting, making connections to self

elaborations.foundational concepts of print, oral, an: concepts include directionality of print, difference between letter and word, difference between writing and drawing, spacing, letter-sound relationship, understanding that pictures convey meaning, taking turns, expressing ideas and needs, and role-playing

elaborations.plan and create stories and other texts: involves experimenting with print and storytelling; supporting communication, including through stories and the use of manipulatives such as puppets, storyboards, digital tools, and toys

curricular_competencies.text: Explore oral storytelling processes

elaborations.oral storytelling processes: creating an original story or finding an existing story (with permission), sharing the story from memory with others, using vocal expression to clarify the meaning of the text

content.text: structure of story

elaborations.structure of story: beginning, middle, end (or first, then, last)

content.text: literary elements and devices

elaborations.literary elements and devices: examples include sound concepts (e.g., rhyme, rhythm, musical, and poetical qualities of language) and humorous and creative texts (e.g., tongue twisters, nursery rhymes, fables, traditional stories)

content.text: reading strategies

elaborations.reading strategies: making meaning using predictions and connections; making meaning from story using pictures, patterns, memory, and prior knowledge; retelling some elements of story; and recognizing familiar words/names and environmental print (e.g., street signs, food packaging)

content.text: oral language strategies

elaborations.oral language strategies: adjusting volume, pace, tone, and articulation; focusing on the speaker; taking turns; asking questions related to the topic; making personal connections; making relevant contributions to discussion

content.text: metacognitive strategies

elaborations.metacognitive strategies: talking and thinking about learning (e.g., through reflecting, questioning, goal setting, self-evaluating) to develop awareness of self as a reader and as a writer

content.text: writing processes
concepts of print

elaborations.concepts of print: the conventional features of written English, such as: the symbolic nature of writing — the correspondence of spoken words to printed words (one-to-one matching) — the association of letters and sounds — the distinctive features of letters and words — the correspondence between uppercase and lowercase letters — left-to-right directionality — the use of space to mark word boundaries — the use of specific signs and symbols for punctuation (e.g., period, exclamation point, question mark) — front and back of a book —

content.text: letter knowledge

elaborations.letter knowledge: recognizing and naming most letters of the alphabet, recognizing most letter-sound matches, recognizing some familiar words

content.text: phonemic and phonological awareness

elaborations.phonemic and phonological awareness: Phonological refers to the sounds of words (as opposed to their meanings): Phonemic awareness is a specific aspect of a learner’s phonological awareness: a child’s ability to segment spoken words into phonemes (e.g., c / a / t) — and to blend phonemes into words indicates a developing phonemic awareness. Phonological awareness involves the abilities to hear and create rhyming words, segment the flow of speech into separate words, and hear syllables as — “chunks” in spoken words.

content.text: letter formation

elaborations.letter formation: the use of scribble writing or letter strings to communicate meaning; distinguishes drawing from writing

content.text: the relationship between reading, writing, and oral language

curricular_competencies.text: Show awareness of how story in First Peoples cultures connects people to family and community

elaborations.story/stories: narrative texts, whether real or imagined, that teach us about human nature, motivation, and experience, and often reflect a personal journey or strengthen a sense of identity. They may also be considered the embodiment of collective wisdom. Stories can be oral, written, or visual, and used to instruct, inspire, and entertain listeners and readers
elaborations.story in first peoples cultures: Traditional and contemporary First Peoples stories take many forms (e.g., prose, song, dance, poetry, theatre, carvings, pictures) and are told for several purposes: teaching (e.g., life lessons, community responsibilities, rites of passage) — sharing creation stories — recording personal, family, and community histories — “mapping” the geography and resources of an area — ensuring cultural continuity (e.g., knowledge of ancestors, language) — healing — entertainment — (from In Our Own Words: Bringing Authentic First Peoples Content to the K–3 Classroom, FNESC/FNSA, 2012)

elaborations.conventions of canadian spelling, gramma: common practices in writing, such as capitals and small letters printed legibly; familiar words spelled correctly; and correct use of periods, question marks, and capitals (including capitalized I); introduction to Canadian spelling

literary elements and devices

elaborations.vocabulary to talk about texts: book, page, chapter, author, title, illustrator, pictures, web page, website, and search box

content.text: reading strategies

elaborations.reading strategies: using illustrations and prior knowledge to predict meaning; rereading; retelling in own words; locating the main idea and details; using knowledge of language patterns and phonics to decode words; identifying familiar and “sight” words; monitoring (asking: Does it look right? Sound right? Make sense?); self-correcting errors consistently using three cueing systems: meaning, structure, and visual

content.text: oral language strategies

elaborations.oral language strategies: adjusting volume, pace, tone, and articulation; focusing on the speaker; taking turns; asking questions related to the topic; making personal connections; and making relevant contributions to discussion

content.text: writing processes

elaborations.writing processes: may include revising, editing, considering audience

content.text: concepts of print

elaborations.concepts of print: the conventional features of written English, such as: the symbolic nature of writing — the correspondence of spoken words to printed words (one-to-one matching) — the association of letters and sounds — the distinctive features of letters and words — the correspondence between uppercase and lowercase letters — left-to-right directionality — the use of space to mark word boundaries — the use of specific signs and symbols for punctuation (e.g., period, exclamation point, question mark) —

content.text: print awareness

elaborations.print awareness: understanding the nature and uses of print, including letters and print symbols; children’s print awareness is closely associated with their word awareness (the ability to recognize words as distinct elements of oral and written communication) and is an important precursor to literacy development and early reading achievement; includes awareness of the differences between letters, words, and sentences

content.text: phonemic and phonological awareness

elaborations.phonemic and phonological awareness: Phonological refers to the sounds of words (as opposed to their meanings): Phonemic awareness is a specific aspect of a learner’s phonological awareness: a child’s ability to segment spoken words into phonemes (e.g., c / a / t) — and to blend phonemes into words indicates a developing phonemic awareness. Phonological awareness involves the abilities to hear and create rhyming words, segment the flow of speech into separate words, and hear syllables as — “chunks” in spoken words.

content.text: letter formation

elaborations.letter formation: legible printing with spacing between letters and words

content.text: sentence structure
conventions

elaborations.conventions: common practices in punctuation (e.g., the use of a period or question mark at end of sentence) and in capitalization (e.g., capitalizing the first letter of the first word at the start of a sentence, people’s names, and the pronoun I)

curricular_competencies.text: Explore and appreciate aspects of First Peoples oral traditions

elaborations.oral traditions: the means by which culture is transmitted over generations other than through written records Among First Peoples, oral tradition may consist of told stories, songs, and other types of distilled wisdom or information, often complemented by dance — or various forms of visual representation, such as carvings or masks. In addition to expressing spiritual and emotional truth (e.g., by symbol and metaphor), it provides a record of literal truth (e.g., about events and — situations). The oral tradition was once integrated into every facet of life of First Peoples and was the basis of the education system. —

elaborations.story/stories: narrative texts, whether real or imagined, that teach us about human nature, motivation, and experience, and often reflect a personal journey or strengthen a sense of identity. They may also be considered the embodiment of collective wisdom. Stories can be oral, written, or visual, and used to instruct, inspire, and entertain listeners and readers. Curricular Competencies – Elaborations Grade 6
elaborations.oral tradition: the means by which culture is transmitted over generations other than through written records Among First Peoples, oral tradition may consist of told stories, songs, and other types of distilled wisdom or information, often complemented by dance — or various forms of visual representation, such as carvings or masks. In addition to expressing spiritual and emotional truth (e.g., by symbol and metaphor), it provides a record of literal truth (e.g., about events — and situations). The oral tradition was once integrated into every facet of life of First Peoples and was the basis of the education system. —

Learning Standards

big_ideas_elaborations.story/stories: narrative texts, whether real or imagined, that teach us about human nature, motivation, and experience, and often reflect a personal journey or strengthen a sense of identity. They may also be considered the embodiment of collective wisdom. Stories can be oral, written, or visual, and used to instruct, inspire, and entertain listeners and readers.
big_ideas_elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —

curricular_competencies.text: Access information and ideas for diverse purposes and from a variety of sources and evaluate their relevance, accuracy, and reliability

elaborations.diverse purposes: examples include to inquire, to explore, to inform, to interpret, to explain, to take a position, to evaluate, to problem solve, to entertain
elaborations.relevance: Students should be prompted to ask: Does it meet the purpose? Is it current? Does it add new information?
elaborations.accuracy: Students should be prompted to distinguish fact from opinion and to consider the source of the information, whether it is supported by evidence, whether it is factually correct, and whether other sources support it.
elaborations.reliability: Students should be prompted to consider the credibility of voice, whether it is a primary or a secondary source, and the trustworthiness and authority of the source.

curricular_competencies.text: Apply appropriate strategies to comprehend written, oral, and visual texts, guide inquiry, and extend thinking

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.inquiry: asking creative and critical questions supported and inspired by texts
elaborations.extend thinking: may include questioning and speculating, acquiring new ideas, analyzing and evaluating ideas, developing explanations, considering alternative points of view, summarizing, synthesizing, and problem solving

curricular_competencies.text: Synthesize ideas from a variety of sources to build understanding
Recognize and appreciate how different features, forms, and genres of texts reflect different purposes, audiences, and messages

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.different features, forms, and genres of: vary depending on the purpose and audience of the text; students should be encouraged to focus on the relationship between form and function (e.g., considering the role in various texts of elements such as negative space in graphic novels; advertisements on websites; lighting and camera angles in film and photography; use of music, paragraph length, line breaks in poetry; silence and intonation in spoken word; and colour)
elaborations.audience/audiences: Students at this level expand their understanding of the range of audiences to include peers, authorities, and technical audiences.

curricular_competencies.text: Think critically, creatively, and reflectively to explore ideas within, between, and beyond texts

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.think critically, creatively, and reflec: questioning, interpreting, comparing, and contrasting a range of texts (e.g., narrative, poetry, visual texts); students should be encouraged to think outside the box, moving beyond the text and comparing texts; useful strategies include “exit slips,” “one star, one wish,” and quick activities to identify thinking

curricular_competencies.text: Recognize and identify the role of personal, social, and cultural contexts, values, and perspectives in texts

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.personal, social, and cultural contexts,: Students should be prompted to recognize the influence of family, friends, activities, education, religion, gender, age, place, settlement patterns, immigration, economic factors, and political events (local and beyond); to recognize that authors write from a perspective influenced by such factors; and to consider the relationship between text and context.

curricular_competencies.text: Recognize how language constructs personal, social, and cultural identity

elaborations.how language constructs personal, social: Our sense of individuality and belonging is a product of the language we use; oral tradition, story, recorded history, and social media; voice; cultural aspects; literacy history; linguistic background (English as first or additional language); register; and language as a system of meaning. Students should recognize that how we use language defines who we are in the world.

curricular_competencies.text: Construct meaningful personal connections between self, text, and world

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —

curricular_competencies.text: Respond to text in personal, creative, and critical ways

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.personal, creative, and critical ways: Students should be prompted to demonstrate comprehension, understanding of connection, and thoughtfulness; support positions with evidence/reasoning; identify and challenge their own assumptions; show awareness of their emotional and cognitive reactions and of their own point of view; and show they can consider texts from different point of views.

curricular_competencies.text: Recognize how literary elements, techniques, and devices enhance and shape meaning

elaborations.how literary elements, techniques, and d: for example, metaphor brings a fresh perspective to the common; irony can add social critique to an argument; allusion suggests connections between diverse elements; form often reflects function; diction influences emotion, persuasiveness, and meaning Curricular Competencies – Elaborations Grade 8

curricular_competencies.text: Recognize an increasing range of text structures and how they contribute to meaning

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —

curricular_competencies.text: Recognize and appreciate the role of story, narrative, and oral tradition in expressing First Peoples perspectives, values, beliefs, and points of view

elaborations.story/stories: narrative texts, whether real or imagined, that teach us about human nature, motivation, and experience, and often reflect a personal journey or strengthen a sense of identity. They may also be considered the embodiment of collective wisdom. Stories can be oral, written, or visual, and used to instruct, inspire, and entertain listeners and readers.

curricular_competencies.text: Develop an awareness of the protocols and ownership associated with First Peoples texts

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.protocols: as applied to local stories, protocols are recognized customs and practices about when and where the stories can be shared, who owns them, and who can share them, because the stories have been passed down through generations

curricular_competencies.text: Exchange ideas and viewpoints to build shared understanding and extend thinking

elaborations.extend thinking: may include questioning and speculating, acquiring new ideas, analyzing and evaluating ideas, developing explanations, considering alternative points of view, summarizing, synthesizing, and problem solving
elaborations.exchange ideas and viewpoints: collaborating in large and small groups through activities such as think-pair-share, debates, four corners, quiet conversation, and lit circles (in which students take on new roles); using active listening skills and receptive body language; paraphrasing and building on others’ ideas; disagreeing respectfully; and extending thinking (e.g., shifting, changing) to broader contexts (social media, digital environments)

curricular_competencies.text: Use writing and design processes to plan, develop, and create engaging and meaningful literary and informational texts for a variety of purposes and audiences

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.literary and informational texts: students should be supported in planning, drafting, and editing multimedia and multimodal texts, such as paragraph compositions that include a theme (subject and author’s opinion) and TAG (title, author, genre); other examples of texts include opinion pieces, poetry, short stories, narratives, slams, spoken word texts, storyboards and comic strips, and masks
elaborations.audience/audiences: Students at this level expand their understanding of the range of audiences to include peers, authorities, and technical audiences.

curricular_competencies.text: Assess and refine texts to improve their clarity, effectiveness, and impact according to purpose, audience, and message

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.audience/audiences: Students at this level expand their understanding of the range of audiences to include peers, authorities, and technical audiences.
elaborations.refine texts: using techniques such as adjusting diction and form according to audience needs and preferences, using verbs effectively, using repetition and substitution for effect, using active instead of passive voice, maintaining parallelism, adding modifiers, replacing be verbs with stronger verbs, varying sentence types, using precise diction, eliminating wordiness

curricular_competencies.text: Use an increasing repertoire of conventions of Canadian spelling, grammar, and punctuation
Use and experiment with oral storytelling processes

elaborations.oral storytelling processes: creating an original story or finding an existing story (with permission), sharing the story from memory with others, using vocal expression to clarify the meaning of the text, using non-verbal communication expressively to clarify the meaning, attending to stage presence, differentiating the storyteller’s natural voice from the characters’ voices, presenting the story efficiently, keeping the listener’s interest throughout, using an expanding repertoire of techniques to enhance audience experience

curricular_competencies.text: Select and use appropriate features, forms, and genres according to audience, purpose, and message

elaborations.audience/audiences: Students at this level expand their understanding of the range of audiences to include peers, authorities, and technical audiences.

curricular_competencies.text: Transform ideas and information to create original texts

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —

content.text: forms, functions, and genres of text

elaborations.genres: literary or thematic categories such as fantasy, humour, adventure, biography
elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, or digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —

content.text: text features

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, or digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.text features: how text and visuals are displayed

content.text: literary elements

elaborations.literary elements: characterization, character types, story structures (e.g., linear, cyclical, iterative), and setting

content.text: literary devices

elaborations.literary devices: sensory detail (e.g., imagery, sound devices), and figurative language (e.g., metaphor, simile, hyperbole)

content.text: elements of visual/graphic texts

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, or digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.elements of visual/graphic texts: may include layout, infographics, emoticons, icons, symbols, interactive visuals, hypertext, and colour, illustration styles (e.g., realism, cartoon, sketch, outline)

content.text: relevance, accuracy, reliability
reading strategies

elaborations.reading strategies: using contextual clues; using phonics and word structure; visualizing; questioning; predicting; previewing text; summarizing; making inferences

content.text: oral language strategies

elaborations.oral language strategies: focusing on the speaker, asking questions to clarify, listening for specifics, expressing opinions, speaking with expression, staying on topic, taking turns

content.text: metacognitive strategies

elaborations.metacognitive strategies: talking and thinking about learning (e.g., through reflecting, questioning, goal setting, self-evaluating) to develop awareness of self as a reader and as a writer

content.text: writing processes

elaborations.writing processes: may include revising, editing, considering audience

content.text: features of oral language

elaborations.features of oral language: including tone, volume, inflection, pace, gestures

content.text: multi-paragraphing

elaborations.multi-paragraphing: developing multi-paragraph compositions that are characterized by unity, development, and coherence

content.text: language usage and context

elaborations.language usage and context: refers to the impact of context on choice of language usage (e.g., when texting, using informal short-form language; when writing an essay, using more formal standard Canadian English)

content.text: elements of style

elaborations.elements of style: diction, figurative language, tone, inclusive language, degree of formality

content.text: syntax and sentence fluency

elaborations.syntax and sentence fluency: use of a mix of simple, compound, and complex sentences; correct pronoun use; subject-verb agreement; use of transitional words; and awareness of run-on sentences and sentence fragments

content.text: conventions

elaborations.conventions: common practices in all standard punctuation use, in capitalization, in quoting, and in Canadian spelling

content.text: presentation techniques

elaborations.presentation techniques: Any presentation (in written, oral, or digital form) should reflect an appropriate choice of medium for the purpose and the audience, and demonstrate thought and care in organization.

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —

curricular_competencies.text: Express an opinion and support it with credible evidence

content.text: forms, functions, and genres of text

elaborations.genres: literary or thematic categories such as fantasy, humour, adventure, biography
elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —

content.text: text features

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.text features: how text and visuals are displayed

content.text: literary elements
literary devices

elaborations.literary devices: sensory detail (e.g., imagery, sound devices); figurative language (e.g., metaphor, simile, hyperbole); irony, paradox, oxymoron

content.text: elements of visual/graphic texts

elaborations.text/texts: Text and texts are generic terms referring to all forms of oral, written, visual, and digital communication: Oral texts include speeches, poems, plays, and oral stories. — Written texts include novels, articles, and short stories. — Visual texts include posters, photographs, and other images. — Digital texts include electronic forms of all the above. — Oral, written, and visual elements can be combined (e.g., in dramatic presentations, graphic novels, films, web pages, advertisements). —
elaborations.elements of visual/graphic texts: layout, infographics, emoticons, icons, symbols, interactive visuals, hypertext, colour; illustration style (realism, cartoon, sketch, outline)

content.text: reading strategies

elaborations.reading strategies: using contextual clues; using phonics and word structure; visualizing; questioning; predicting; previewing text; summarizing; making inferences

content.text: oral language strategies

elaborations.oral language strategies: focusing on the speaker, asking questions to clarify, listening for specifics, expressing opinions, speaking with expression, staying on topic, taking turns

content.text: metacognitive strategies

elaborations.metacognitive strategies: talking and thinking about learning (e.g., through reflecting, questioning, goal setting, self-evaluating) to develop awareness of self as a reader and as a writer

content.text: writing processes

elaborations.writing processes: may include revising, editing, considering audience

content.text: features of oral language

elaborations.features of oral language: such as tone, volume, inflection, pace, gestures

content.text: multi-paragraphing

elaborations.multi-paragraphing: developing multi-paragraph compositions that are characterized by unity, development, and coherence

content.text: language change

elaborations.language change: Languages change slowly but continually (e.g., Old English to Modern English): Changes are evident in different dialects. — New words and new ways of saying things emerge as culture and society change. — New media accelerates change. —

content.text: elements of style

elaborations.elements of style: diction, figurative language, tone, inclusive language, and degree of formality

content.text: usage

elaborations.usage: such as avoiding double negatives, mixed metaphors, malapropisms, word misuse

content.text: syntax and sentence fluency

elaborations.syntax and sentence fluency: use of a mix of simple, compound, and complex sentences; correct pronoun use; subject-verb agreement; use of transitional words; awareness of run-on sentences and sentence fragments

content.text: conventions

elaborations.conventions: common practices in all standard punctuation use, in capitalization, in quoting, and in Canadian spelling

content.text: presentation techniques

elaborations.presentation techniques: Any presentation (in written, oral, or digital form) should reflect an appropriate choice of medium for the purpose and the audience, and demonstrate thought and care in organization.

content.text: rhetorical devices

elaborations.rhetorical devices: figurative language, parallelism, repetition, irony, humour, exaggeration, emotional language, logic, direct address, rhetorical questions, and allusion

content.text: connotation and denotation

elaborations.place: Place is any environment, locality, or context with which people interact to learn, create memory, reflect on history, connect with culture, and establish identity. The connection between people and place is foundational to First Peoples perspectives of the world. Key questions about place: — What is place? — What are some ways in which people experience place? — How can you gain a sense of place in your local environment? — How can you share your observations and ideas about living things in your local environment to help someone else learn about place?

content.text: basic needs of plants and animals

elaborations.basic needs: include habitat — food, water, shelter, and space
elaborations.plants: features may include roots, stems, leaves, flowers, seeds
elaborations.animals: features may include shape, size, feet, teeth, body covering, eyes, ears

content.text: adaptations of local plants and animals

elaborations.adaptations: may include structural features or behaviours that allow organisms to survive
elaborations.plants: features may include roots, stems, leaves, flowers, seeds
elaborations.animals: features may include shape, size, feet, teeth, body covering, eyes, ears

content.text: local First Peoples uses of plants and animals

elaborations.plants: features may include roots, stems, leaves, flowers, seeds
elaborations.animals: features may include shape, size, feet, teeth, body covering, eyes, ears
elaborations.local first peoples uses: First Peoples practice and knowledge of plant and animal use (e.g., local berries or food, plants and animals, conservation of resources)

content.text: properties of familiar materials

elaborations.properties: colour, texture (smooth or rough), flexibility (bendable or stretchable), hardness, lustre (shiny or dull), absorbency, etc.
elaborations.familiar materials: fabric, wood, plastic, glass, metal/foil, sand, etc.

content.text: effects of pushes/pulls on movement

elaborations.effects of pushes/pulls: how things move (e.g., bounce, roll, slide)

content.text: effects of size, shape, and materials on movement
weather changes

elaborations.weather: — temperature: cold, hot, cool, warm — cloud cover: clear, cloudy, partly cloudy, foggy — precipitation: rain, snow, hail, freezing rain — wind: calm, breezy, windy

content.text: seasonal changes

elaborations.seasonal changes: — seasons: spring, summer, fall, winter — plant life cycle

content.text: living things make changes to accommodate daily and seasonal cycles

elaborations.living things make changes: living things may make physical and behavioural changes to survive in different conditions (e.g., migration, hibernation, etc.)

content.text: First Peoples knowledge of seasonal changes

elaborations.seasonal changes: — seasons: spring, summer, fall, winter — plant life cycle

content.text: classification of living and non-living things

elaborations.classification: — Is it living or non-living? Is it a plant, animal or something else? — differences between conventional scientific and indigenous ways of classifying

content.text: names of local plants and animals
structural features of living things in the local environment

elaborations.structural features: How do stems, roots, leaves, skeleton or no skeleton or exoskeleton, lots of legs, few legs, eyes, etc. help us understand organisms?

content.text: behavioural adaptations of animals in the local environment

elaborations.behavioural adaptations: dormancy, hibernation, nesting, migration, catching food, camouflage (stick bugs), mimicry (fly that looks like bee), territorialism (squirrels fighting), etc.

content.text: specific properties of materials allow us to use them in different ways

elaborations.specific properties: — solids keep shape; liquids and gases flow — properties of local materials determine use by First Peoples (local examples: cedar for canoes, mountain goat horns used as spoons, etc.)

content.text: natural and artificial sources of light and sound

elaborations.sources of light: natural sources include the sun; artificial sources include light bulbs
elaborations.sound: — examples: pitch, tone, volume — ways of making, recording, and transmitting sound, etc.

content.text: properties of light and sound depend on their source and the objects with which they interact

elaborations.sound: — examples: pitch, tone, volume — ways of making, recording, and transmitting sound, etc.
elaborations.properties of light: — examples: brightness, colour — objects are made visible by radiating their own light or being illuminated by reflected light — interactions of light with different objects create images and shadows Content – Elaborations Grade 1 — light interactions can make plants grow, make shadows, or cause sunburn, depending on the source and location (seasons depend on light from the sun and how spread out the sun’s rays are) — plants grow toward light

content.text: common objects in the sky

elaborations.common objects in the sky: — the appearance of the moon and stars at night — sunrise/set, moonrise/set — the sun and the moon are important in different cultures, with respect to customs and traditions

content.text: the knowledge of First Peoples shared First Peoples knowledge of the sky local First Peoples knowledge of the local landscape, plants and animals local First Peoples understanding and use of seasonal rounds

elaborations.local first peoples: e.g., may include oral history with Elder—origins and local stories
elaborations.seasonal rounds: Seasonal rounds refers to a pattern of movement from one resource-gathering area to another in a cycle that is followed each year

content.text: local patterns that occur on Earth and in the sky

elaborations.local patterns: the relationship of local weather to the four seasons in terms of temperature, cloud cover, precipitation, and wind

elaborations.place: Place is any environment, locality, or context with which people interact to learn, create memory, reflect on history, connect with culture, and establish identity. The connection between people and place is foundational to First Peoples perspectives of the world. Key questions about place: — What is place? — What are some ways in which people experience place? — How can you gain a sense of place in your local environment? — How can you share your observations and ideas about living things in your local environment to help someone else learn about place?

content.text: metamorphic and non-metamorphic life cycles of different organisms

elaborations.metamorphic: metamorphic life cycles: body structure changes (e.g., caterpillar to butterfly, mealworm transformation, tadpoles to frog)
elaborations.non-metamorphic: non-metamorphic life cycles: organism keeps same body structure through life but size changes (e.g., humans)

content.text: similarities and differences between offspring and parent

elaborations.offspring and parent: a kitten looks like cat and a puppy looks like dog but they do change as they grow; salmon change a great deal as they grow and need fresh and salt water environments to survive

content.text: First Peoples use of their knowledge of life cycles

elaborations.first peoples use of their knowledge: — stewardship: sustainably gathering plants and hunting/fishing in response to seasons and animal migration patterns (e.g., clam gardens, seasonal rounds, etc.) — sustainable fish hatchery programs run by local First Peoples

content.text: physical ways of changing materials

elaborations.physical: physical ways of changing materials: — warming, cooling, cutting, bending, stirring, mixing — materials may be combined or physically changed to be used in different ways (e.g., plants can be ground up and combined with other materials to make dyes)

content.text: chemical ways of changing materials

elaborations.chemical: chemical ways of changing materials: cooking, burning, etc.

content.text: types of forces

elaborations.forces: — contact forces and at-a-distance forces: (cid:1) different types of magnets (cid:1) static electricity Content – Elaborations Grade 2 — balanced and unbalanced forces: (cid:1) the way different objects fall depending on their shape (air resistance) (cid:1) the way objects move over/in different materials (water, air, ice, snow) (cid:1) the motion caused by different strengths of forces

content.text: water sources including local watersheds

elaborations.water sources: — oceans, lakes, rivers, wells, springs — the majority of fresh water is stored underground and in glaciers

content.text: water conservation

elaborations.water conservation: fresh water is a limited resource and is not being replaced at the same rate as it is being used

content.text: the water cycle

elaborations.water cycle: The water cycle is driven by the sun and includes evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and runoff. The water cycle is also a major component of weather (e.g., precipitation, clouds)

content.text: local First People’s knowledge of water: water cycles conservation connection to other systems

elaborations.connection to other systems: cultural significance of water (i.e., water is essential for all interconnected forms of life)

big_ideas_elaborations.interconnectedness means that all things: things demonstrate interconnectedness? All matter is made of particles.
big_ideas_elaborations.how are matter and energy related?: Big Ideas – Elaborations Grade 3 Thermal energy can be produced and transferred.
big_ideas_elaborations.how is thermal energy transferred betwee: Wind, water, and ice change the shape of the land.

The connection between people and place is foundational to First Peoples perspectives of the world. Key questions about place: — How does what you know about place affect your observations, questions, and predictions? — How does understanding place help you analyze information and recognize connections and relationships in your local environment? — How does place connect with stewardship? — How can you be a steward in your local environment?

content.text: biodiversity in the local environment

elaborations.biodiversity: — biodiversity: the variety of different types of living things in an ecosystem — characteristics of local plants, animals and fungi

content.text: the knowledge of local First Peoples of ecosystems

elaborations.the knowledge of local first peoples: the interconnection between living and non-living things in the local environment; our shared responsibility to care for the local environment (i.e., stewardship); information shared from the local First Peoples community and Elders Content – Elaborations Grade 3
elaborations.ecosystems: — population: all the members of the same type of living thing (species) in an area — communities: different populations in an area living together

content.text: energy is needed for life

elaborations.energy is needed for life: — producers (plants), consumers (animals), and decomposers (bacteria and fungi) respond to their environment in energy pyramids (flow of energy in the community from the sun) — food chains: the flow of food energy from one organism to another (e.g., grass to rabbit to lynx) — food webs: interconnecting food chains (e.g., a rabbit may be eaten by a lynx or a wolf)

content.text: matter is anything that has mass and takes up space
atoms are building blocks of matter
sources of thermal energy

elaborations.sources: thermal energy can be produced by chemical reactions (e.g., hand warmers), friction between moving objects, the sun, etc.
elaborations.thermal energy: the energy that comes from the movement of particles within matter

content.text: transfer of thermal energy

elaborations.thermal energy: the energy that comes from the movement of particles within matter
elaborations.transfer of thermal energy: — conduction (touching — e.g., hold an ice cube) — convection (current — why do we hang mittens over a heat source?) — radiation (through space by a wave — e.g., heat from the sun)

content.text: major local landforms

elaborations.landforms: mountains, hills, plateaus, valleys, riverbeds, deltas, glaciers, etc.; oral narrative about landforms

content.text: local First Peoples knowledge of local landforms

elaborations.landforms: mountains, hills, plateaus, valleys, riverbeds, deltas, glaciers, etc.; oral narrative about landforms

content.text: observable changes in the local environment caused by erosion and deposition by wind, water, and ice

big_ideas_elaborations.how is sensing and responding related to: Matter has mass, takes up space, and can change phase.
big_ideas_elaborations.how does heating and cooling affect phas: Big Ideas – Elaborations Grade 4 Energy can be transformed.
big_ideas_elaborations.what is the relationship between energy: The motions of Earth and the moon cause observable patterns that affect living and non-living systems.

content.text: sensing and responding: humans other animals plants

elaborations.other animals: e.g., echolocation, UV sensors, magnetoreception, infrared sensing, etc.
elaborations.plants: e.g., response to light, touch, water, gravity, etc.

content.text: biomes as large regions with similar environmental features

elaborations.biomes: biomes are regions grouped by similar temperature and precipitation (e.g., climate: long-term weather patterns) — terrestrial biomes — aquatic/marine biomes Content – Elaborations Grade 4

content.text: phases of matter
the effect of temperature on particle movement

elaborations.effect of temperature: solids, liquids, and gases change with heating (e.g., boiling point, melting point [melting chocolate]) and cooling (e.g., freezing point [making ice cream]), and these physical changes are reversible

content.text: energy: has various forms is conserved

elaborations.various forms: energy can be described in these ways: the energy of motion (kinetic), light, sound, thermal, elastic, nuclear, chemical, magnetic, , gravitational, and electrical
elaborations.conserved: the law of conservation of energy — energy cannot be created or destroyed but can be changed

content.text: devices that transform energy

elaborations.devices that transform energy: devices that transform energy change input energy into a different output energy (e.g., glow stick [chemical to light], wind-up toy [elastic to mechanical], flashlight [electrical to light])

content.text: local changes caused by Earth’s axis, rotation, and orbit

elaborations.earth’s axis, rotation, and orbit: Earth’s axis, rotation, and orbit cause changes locally: — day and night: animals are nocturnal (active at night) and diurnal (active during day) — annual seasons: plants and animals respond to the seasons (drop leaves, change colour)

content.text: the effects of the relative positions of the sun, moon, and Earth including local First Peoples perspectives

elaborations.the effects of the relative positions of: — phases of the moon, tides, etc. — tides affect living organisms — lunar and solar eclipses
elaborations.local first peoples perspectives: teachings and stories about the sun and the moon

big_ideas_elaborations.what are their uses?: Machines are devices that transfer force and energy.
big_ideas_elaborations.what natural machines can you identify i: Earth materials change as they move through the rock cycle and can be used as natural resources.

elaborations.place: Place is any environment, locality, or context with which people interact to learn, create memory, reflect on history, connect with culture, and establish identity. The connection between people and place is foundational to First Peoples perspectives of the world. Key questions about place: — How does place influence your ability to plan and conduct an inquiry? — How does your understanding of place affect the ways in which you collect evidence and evaluate it? — How do the place-based experiences and stories of others affect the ways in which you communicate your findings and other information? — Ways of knowing refers to the various beliefs about the nature of knowledge that people have; they can include, but are not limited to, Aboriginal, gender-related, subject/discipline specific, cultural, embodied and intuitive beliefs about knowledge. What are the connections between ways of knowing and place?

content.text: basic structures and functions of body systems: digestive musculo-skeletal respiratory circulatory
solutions and solubility

elaborations.solutions and solubility: — solutions (e.g., apple juice, coffee) that can be separated through distillation, evaporation, and crystallization — solubility of solids, liquids, and gases (e.g., salt [solid], honey [liquid], carbon dioxide [gas in water makes pop]) — properties of solutions: concentration, pH, etc. — dissolving: process of forming a solution

content.text: properties of simple machines and their force effects

elaborations.simple machines: levers, wedge, inclined plane, wheel and axle, pulley, and screw
elaborations.force effects: force effects include changing direction and multiplying force

content.text: machines: constructed found in nature

elaborations.constructed: combinations of simple machines form complex machines
elaborations.found in nature: the lever is the basis of nearly every aspect of the musculoskeletal system

content.text: power – the rate at which energy is transferred

elaborations.power: examples include students racing up a hill, machine power ratings, motors

content.text: the rock cycle
local types of earth materials

elaborations.earth materials: include mineral, rock, clay, boulder, gravel, sand, soil

content.text: First Peoples concepts of interconnectedness in the environment

elaborations.interconnectedness: everything in the environment is one/connected (e.g., sun, sky, plants and animals) and we have a responsibility to care for them

content.text: the nature of sustainable practices around BC’s resources
First Peoples knowledge of sustainable practices

big_ideas_elaborations.how are balanced and unbalanced forces e: The solar system is part of the Milky Way, which is one of billions of galaxies.

content.text: the basic structures and functions of body systems: excretory reproductive hormonal nervous

elaborations.systems: First People’s understandings of body systems in humans and animals
elaborations.hormonal: chemical messengers in the body (e.g., insulin, adrenalin)
elaborations.nervous: brain, spinal cord, etc.; role of receptors — the brain interprets the signals received and can make mistakes (e.g., optical illusions) in those interpretations

content.text: heterogeneous mixtures

elaborations.heterogeneous mixtures: suspensions (e.g., salad dressing), emulsions (e.g., milk), colloids (e.g., aerosols)

content.text: mixtures: separated using a difference in component properties local First Peoples knowledge of separation and extraction methods

elaborations.separated using a difference in componen: — density (e.g., centrifuge or settling, silt deposits in a river delta, tailings ponds, Roman aqueduct settling sections) — particle size (e.g., sieves, filters)
elaborations.local first peoples knowledge: historical and current First Peoples use of separation and extraction methods (e.g., eulachon oil, extraction of medicines from plants, pigments, etc.)

content.text: Newton’s three laws of motion

elaborations.newton’s three laws of motion: — first law: objects will stay stopped or in constant motion until acted upon by an outside force — second law: only an unbalanced force causes acceleration — third law: every force has an equal and opposite reaction force

content.text: effects of balanced and unbalanced forces in daily physical activities

elaborations.balanced and unbalanced forces: — balanced forces are equal and opposite forces (e.g., sitting in a chair) — unbalanced forces are unequal; one force is larger (e.g., race cars on different ramps, mousetrap cars, rockets)
elaborations.daily physical activities: examples of effects of balanced and unbalanced forces in school sports and physical education activities

content.text: force of gravity

elaborations.force of gravity: — gravity is the force of attraction between objects that pulls all objects toward each other — on Earth, gravity pulls objects toward the centre of the planet (e.g., falling objects, egg drop)

elaborations.place: Place is any environment, locality, or context with which people interact to learn, create memory, reflect on history, connect with culture, and establish identity. The connection between people and place is foundational to First Peoples perspectives of the world. Key questions about place: — How does place inform your questions and inquiries? — How does place influence your ability to plan and conduct an inquiry and make predictions about outcomes? — How does your understanding of place affect the ways in which you collect evidence and evaluate it? — As you consider the significance, worth, or value of an outcome or finding, how can you show different ways of knowing? — How can your understanding of place influence project designs? — How do the place-based experiences and stories of others affect the ways in which you communicate and collaborate?

content.text: organisms have evolved over time

elaborations.organisms have evolved over time: change in traits of populations over time

content.text: survival needs

elaborations.survival needs: all organisms need space, food, water, and access to resources in order to survive

content.text: natural selection

elaborations.natural selection: the natural process by which certain traits that have a greater fitness for their environment lead to a reproductive advantage; this process happens within a population over time because of genetic variation

content.text: elements and compounds are pure substances

elaborations.elements: a pure substance consisting of a single type of atom, as distinguished by its atomic number (e.g., iron, copper)
elaborations.compounds: a pure substance consisting of two or more different atoms held together in a defined special arrangement by chemical bonds (e.g., water/salt)
elaborations.pure substances: matter that consists of only one type of particle and has one set of properties (e.g., density, boiling point, solubility, conductivity)

content.text: crystalline structure of solids

elaborations.crystalline structure: crystals formed by a unique arrangement of particles (e.g., rock candy, quartz, snowflakes)

content.text: chemical changes

elaborations.chemical changes: when atoms rearrange into new products accompanied by an energy change (e.g., rusting, the reaction of vinegar and baking soda, etc.)

content.text: electricity generated in different ways with different environmental impacts electromagnetism

elaborations.generated in different ways: ways of generating electricity including the use of wind, water, coal, geothermal, and solar energy
elaborations.electromagnetism: — the electromagnetic force is responsible for both electricity and magnetism — moving or changing a magnetic field relative to a wire produces electric current (e.g., electricity generation by a turbine) — an electric current passing through a wire produces a magnetic field (e.g., constructing a simple electromagnet using a wire, iron nail and battery)

content.text: the fossil record provides evidence for changes in biodiversity over geological time

elaborations.geological time: — the geologic time scale categorizes the time periods of Earth’s geologic history — ages of rocks and fossils can be determined by both relative and absolute methods

content.text: First Peoples knowledge of changes in biodiversity over time
evidence of climate change over geological time and the recent impacts of humans: physical records local First Peoples knowledge of climate change

elaborations.geological time: — the geologic time scale categorizes the time periods of Earth’s geologic history — ages of rocks and fossils can be determined by both relative and absolute methods
elaborations.climate change: change in climate affects: — the interconnectedness of plants and animals, and their local environment — e.g., changes to harvesting dates, changes to schedules due to early/later ripening and runs, lowered water levels in creeks, rivers and lakes, change in humidity impacts the ability to preserve salmon, etc.
elaborations.impacts of humans: — humans are capable of changing Earth’s landscape, climate, and systems — efficacy of sustainable practices
elaborations.physical records: ice flow data, fossil record, etc.
elaborations.local first peoples knowledge of climate: oral history, change in traditional practice (e.g., the timing of harvest has been impacted by climate change), etc.

content.text: characteristics of life

elaborations.characteristics of life: living things respire, grow, take in nutrients, produce waste, respond to stimuli, and reproduce; there is debate as to whether or not to classify viruses as living things

content.text: cell theory and types of cells

elaborations.cell theory: — living things are made of one or more cells — all cells come from pre-existing cells — the cell is a basic unit of life
elaborations.types of cells: — prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells — plant and animal cells — cells contain structures that carry out essential functions
elaborations.types: types of electromagnetic radiation: the electromagnetic spectrum consists of radio, microwave, infrared, light, UV, X-ray, and gamma rays

content.text: photosynthesis and cellular respiration
the relationship of micro-organisms with living things: basic functions of the immune system vaccination and antibiotics impacts of epidemics and pandemics on human populations

elaborations.micro-organisms: — micro-organisms are key to nutrient recycling in ecosystems as they act as decomposers — viruses and bacteria can cause disease and can also be used in industry (e.g., production of cheese and salami) and agriculture (e.g., production of striped tulips)
elaborations.immune system: — the immune system provides a barrier to infections and a number of non-specific and specific responses to fight infection (e.g., fever, antibodies, phagocytes, inflammation) — different populations have greater immunity to certain infections than other populations (e.g., impact of smallpox epidemic on First Peoples)
elaborations.vaccination: vaccination can prevent the spread of infectious disease
elaborations.antibiotics: antibiotics are effective only against living organisms, such as bacteria, and not against viruses; overuse of antibiotics can lead to the development of antibiotic-resistant strains of bacteria (“superbugs”)

content.text: kinetic molecular theory (KMT)
atomic theory and models

elaborations.atomic theory: provides evidence for the existence of atoms and molecules
elaborations.models: models can be used to represent: — the arrangement and motion of particles in different phases — the arrangement of and forces that bind protons, neutrons, and electrons in an atom — the quarks and leptons in protons, neutrons, and electrons

content.text: protons, neutrons, and quarks

elaborations.protons, neutrons, and quarks: protons and neutrons (made of quarks) are held together in the nucleus by a strong nuclear force

content.text: electrons and leptons

elaborations.electrons and leptons: electrons (a type of lepton) are held at a distance from the nucleus through electromagnetism

content.text: types and effects of electromagnetic radiation

elaborations.types: types of electromagnetic radiation: the electromagnetic spectrum consists of radio, microwave, infrared, light, UV, X-ray, and gamma rays
elaborations.effects: effects of electromagnetic radiation: positive effects include cancer treatments; negative effects include sunburns Content – Elaborations Grade 8

content.text: light: properties behaviours ways of sensing

elaborations.properties: properties of light — acts like both a wave and a particle — wavelength, amplitude, frequency
elaborations.behaviours: behaviours of light: — reflection, refraction, absorption, transmission, scattering — images formed by lenses and mirrors — effects of translucent, transparent, and opaque objects
elaborations.ways of sensing: ways of sensing light: human vision, optical instruments, cameras

elaborations.plate tectonic movement: — types of plate movements — plate boundaries — earthquakes and volcanoes

content.text: asexual reproduction: mitosis different forms

elaborations.mitosis: the process through which pre-existing cells make two identical copies of themselves
elaborations.different forms: different forms of asexual reproduction: fission, budding, cloning, spores, grafting

content.text: sexual reproduction: meiosis human sexual reproduction

elaborations.meiosis: the process through which sex cells (eggs and sperm) are formed by the dividing of a parent cell twice, resulting in four daughter cells
elaborations.human sexual reproduction: the result of humans having two parents is that offspring are not genetically identical to either parent

content.text: element properties as organized in the periodic table

elaborations.periodic table: The periodic table groups elements according to their atomic number and properties (e.g., atomic size, metals/non-metals/semi-metals, chemical families, diatomic elements).

content.text: The arrangement of electrons determines the compounds formed by elements

elaborations.compounds: — ionic and covalent — names and formulas

content.text: circuits — must be complete for electrons to flow

elaborations.circuits: — basic components include power source, load/resistor (lightbulbs, etc.), conductor and switch — types of circuits include series, parallel, short circuits — current flow in a circuit: alternating current (AC) and direct current (DC)

content.text: voltage, current, and resistance

elaborations.voltage, current, and resistance: voltage, current, and resistance are related: — Ohm’s Law (V=IR) — relative dangers of current and voltage

content.text: effects of solar radiation on the cycling of matter and energy

elaborations.effects of solar radiation: solar radiation provides the energy required for most life on Earth, and is the root cause of wind and ocean currents, which distribute energy and nutrients around the planet, as well as the energy sources for the water cycle

content.text: matter cycles within biotic and abiotic components of ecosystems

elaborations.matter cycles: — e.g., water, nitrogen, carbon, phosphorous, etc. — human impacts on sources and sinks (e.g., climate change, deforestation, agriculture, etc.) — bioaccumulation and biomagnification

content.text: sustainability of systems

elaborations.sustainability of systems: a systems approach to sustainability sees all matter and energy as interconnected and existing in dynamic equilibrium (e.g., carbon as a key factor in climate change, greenhouse effect, water cycle, etc.)
elaborations.sustainability: First Peoples perspectives on sustainability of systems

content.text: First Peoples knowledge of interconnectedness and sustainability

elaborations.interconnectedness: everything is connected, from local to global; First Peoples perspectives on interconnectedness
elaborations.sustainability: First Peoples perspectives on sustainability of systems

curricular_competencies.text: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills to ask questions; gather, interpret, and analyze ideas; and communicate findings and decisions
Develop a plan of action to address a selected problem or issue
Construct arguments defending the significance of individuals/groups, places, events, or developments (significance)
Ask questions, corroborate inferences, and draw conclusions about the content and origins of a variety of sources, including mass media (evidence)
Sequence objects, images, or events, and recognize the positive and negative aspects of continuities and changes in the past and present (continuity and change)
Differentiate between short- and long-term causes, and intended and unintended consequences, of events, decisions, or developments (cause and consequence)
Take stakeholders’ perspectives on issues, developments, or events by making inferences about their beliefs, values, and motivations (perspective)
Make ethical judgments about events, decisions, or actions that consider the conditions of a particular time and place, and assess appropriate ways to respond (ethical judgment)

content.text: elements in the arts, including but not limited to: dance: body, space, dynamics, time, relationships, form drama: character, time, place, plot music: beat/pulse, rhythm, tempo, pitch, dynamics visual arts: elements of design: line, shape, texture, colour; principles of design: pattern, repetition

elaborations.dance: the elements of dance are universally present in all dance forms and grow in sophistication over time.
elaborations.music: the elements of music are universally present in all forms of vocal and instrumental music and grow in sophistication over time.
elaborations.body: what the body is doing, including whole or partial body action, types of movement (locomotor and non-locomotor), etc.
elaborations.space: where the body is moving, including place, level, direction, pathway, size/reach, shape, etc.
elaborations.time: how the body moves in relation to time, including beat (underlying pulse), tempo, and rhythmic patterns
elaborations.relationships: with whom or what the body is moving; movement happens in a variety of relationship including pairs, groups, objects, and environments
elaborations.form: The shape or structure of a dance; the orderly arrangement of thematic material. For example: phrase, beginning, middle, end, ABA, canon, call and response, narrative, abstract
elaborations.character: in drama, taking on and exploring the thoughts, perceptions, feelings, and beliefs of another
elaborations.rhythm: the arrangement of sounds and silences over time
elaborations.tempo: the frequency or speed of the beat
elaborations.pitch: how high or low a note is Content – Elaborations Kindergarten
elaborations.line: e.g., thick, thin, wavy, zigzag, jagged, etc.
elaborations.shape: 2-dimensional enclosed space, as compared to form which is 3-dimensional
elaborations.texture: the way something feels (e.g., smooth, rough, fuzzy)
elaborations.principles of design: the planned use of the visual elements to achieve a desired effect
elaborations.pattern: a design in which shapes, colours or lines repeat with regularity
elaborations.repetition: using the same object, colour, marking, or type of line more than once

content.text: processes, materials, movements, technologies, tools and techniques to support arts activities

elaborations.technologies: includes both manual and digital technologies (e.g., electronic media, production elements, information technology, sound equipment and recording technologies, etc.); in visual arts, any visual image-making technology (e.g., paintbrush, scissors, pencil, stamp) and includes the improvisational use of miscellaneous items

content.text: notation to represent sounds, ideas and movement

elaborations.notation: any written, visual, or kinetic form of representing music compositions; for example, movement and body percussion (e.g., clap, snap, stomp) can be used to investigate and represent music patterns and concepts; in dance, this can include written formal and informal systems of symbols, shapes, and lines that represent body position and movement

content.text: a variety of dramatic forms

elaborations.dramatic forms: a medium for the expression of dramatic meaning (e.g., improvisation, tableau, role-play, mime, readers theatre, story theatre); may involve the integration of a variety of media and a combination of the arts

content.text: symbolism as expressions of meaning

elaborations.symbolism: use of objects, words, or actions to represent abstract ideas; includes but is not limited to colours, images, movements, and sounds (e.g., love can be symbolized by the colour red or the cradling of one’s arms)

content.text: traditional and contemporary Aboriginal arts and arts- making processes

elaborations.aboriginal arts: dances, songs, stories, and objects created by Aboriginal peoples for use in daily life or to serve a purpose inspired by ceremonies or rituals as part of cultural tradition

content.text: variety of local works of art and artistic traditions

elaborations.works of art: the results of creative processes in disciplines such as dance, drama, music, and visual arts

elaborations.personal and collective responsibility: ensuring the physical and emotional safety of self and others when engaging in the arts; being considerate of sensitive content, facilities, and materials
elaborations.sharing: includes any form of presentation as outlined in the Connecting, Creating, Presenting, and Responding in Arts Education resource

elaborations.music: the elements of music are universally present in all forms of vocal and instrumental music and grow in sophistication over time.
elaborations.body: what the body is doing, including whole or partial body action, types of movement (locomotor and non-locomotor), etc.
elaborations.space: where the body is moving, including place, level, direction, pathway, size/reach, shape, etc.
elaborations.time: how the body moves in relation to time, including beat (underlying pulse), tempo, and rhythmic patterns
elaborations.relationships: with whom or what the body is moving; movement happens in a variety of relationship including pairs, groups, objects, and environments
elaborations.form: The shape or structure of a dance; the orderly arrangement of thematic material. For example: phrase, beginning, middle, end, ABA, canon, call and response, narrative, abstract
elaborations.character: in drama, taking on and exploring the thoughts, perceptions, feelings, and beliefs of another
elaborations.duration: the length of a sound or silence in relation to the beat (e.g., shorter, longer, equal) Content – Elaborations Grade 3
elaborations.tempo: the frequency or speed of the beat
elaborations.pitch: how high or low a note is
elaborations.timbre: the characteristic quality of a sound independent of pitch and dynamics; tone colour
elaborations.texture: simultaneous layering of sounds (e.g., combining singing with other instruments, partner songs)
elaborations.principles of design: the planned use of the visual elements to achieve a desired effect
elaborations.pattern: a design in which shapes, colours or lines repeat with regularity
elaborations.repetition: using the same object, colour, marking, or type of line more than once

content.text: processes, materials, technologies, tools, and techniques to support arts activities

elaborations.technologies: includes both manual and digital technologies (e.g., electronic media, production elements, information technology, sound equipment and recording technologies, etc.); in visual arts, any visual image-making technology (e.g., paintbrush, scissors, pencil, stamp) and includes the improvisational use of miscellaneous items

content.text: notation to represent sounds, ideas, and movement

elaborations.notation: any written, visual, or kinetic form of representing music compositions; for example, non-traditional invented notation can be used to represent sound, or traditional notation used to represent high/low pitches on a three-lined staff; in dance, this can include written formal and informal systems of symbols, shapes, and lines that represent body position and movement

content.text: a variety of dramatic forms

elaborations.dramatic forms: a medium for the expression of dramatic meaning (e.g., improvisation, tableau, role-play, mime, readers theatre, story theatre); may involve the integration of a variety of media and a combination of the arts

content.text: image development strategies

elaborations.image development strategies: processes that transform ideas and experiences into visual images (e.g., elaboration, repetition, and simplification.)

content.text: choreographic devices

elaborations.choreographic devices: ways of developing movement (e.g., change level, dynamics, time, size, repetition)

content.text: symbolism as ways of creating and representing meaning

elaborations.symbolism: use of an object, word, or action to represent an abstract idea; includes but is not limited to colours, images, movements, and sounds (e.g., the solar system can be represented through positive and negative space, sound exploration, or collaborative movement)

content.text: traditional and contemporary Aboriginal arts and arts- making processes

elaborations.aboriginal arts: dances, songs, stories, and objects created by Aboriginal peoples for use in daily life or to serve a purpose inspired by ceremonies as part of cultural tradition

content.text: a variety of local works of art and artistic traditions from diverse cultures, communities, times, and places

elaborations.works of art: the results of creative processes in disciplines such as dance, drama, music, and visual arts

content.text: personal and collective responsibility associated with creating, experiencing, or sharing in a safe learning environment

elaborations.personal and collective responsibility: ensuring the physical and emotional safety of self and others when engaging in the arts; being considerate of sensitive content, facilities, and materials
elaborations.sharing: includes any form of presentation as outlined in the Connecting, Creating, Presenting, and Responding in Arts Education resource

curricular_competencies.text: Choose artistic elements, processes, materials, movements, technologies, tools, techniques and environments using combinations and selections for specific purposes in art making

elaborations.elements: characteristics of dance, drama, music, and visual arts

curricular_competencies.text: Create artistic works collaboratively and as an individual using ideas inspired by imagination, inquiry, experimentation, and purposeful play

elaborations.purposeful play: learning that uses real-life and/or imaginary situations to engage and challenge learners’ thinking. Through planned purposeful play, students express their natural curiosity while exploring the world around them. It also provides a means for high-level reasoning and problem solving in a variety of ways. Learning can be performed collaboratively in both solo and ensemble contexts.
