elaborations.dance: the elements of dance are universally present in all dance forms and grow in sophistication over time.
elaborations.music: the elements of music are universally present in all forms of vocal and instrumental music and grow in sophistication over time.
elaborations.body: what the body is doing, including whole or partial body action, types of movement (locomotor and non-locomotor), etc.

elaborations.relationships: with whom or what the body is moving; movement happens in a variety of relationship including pairs, groups, objects, and environments

elaborations.pattern: a design in which shapes, colours or lines repeat with regularity
elaborations.repetition: using the same object, colour, marking, or type of line more than once

content.text: manipulation of elements and principles to create meaning in the arts, including but not limited to: dance: body, space, dynamics, time, relationships, form, and movement principles drama: character, time, place, plot, tension, mood, focus, contrast music: beat/pulse, metre, duration, rhythm, tempo, pitch, timbre, dynamics, form, texture, notation visual arts: elements of design: line, shape, space, texture, colour, form, value; principles of design: pattern, repetition, balance, contrast, emphasis, rhythm, movement, variety, proportion, unity, harmony

elaborations.dance: the elements of dance are universally present in all dance forms and grow in sophistication over time.
elaborations.music: the elements of music are universally present in all forms of vocal and instrumental music and grow in sophistication over time.
elaborations.body: what the body is doing, including whole or partial body action, types of movement (locomotor and non-locomotor), etc.
elaborations.space: where the body is moving, including place, level, direction, pathway, size/reach, shape, etc.
elaborations.time: how the body moves in relation to time, including beat (underlying pulse), tempo, and rhythmic patterns
elaborations.relationships: with whom or what the body is moving; movement happens in a variety of relationship including pairs, groups, objects, and environments
elaborations.form: The shape or structure of a dance; the orderly arrangement of thematic material. For example: phrase, beginning, middle, end, ABA, canon, call and response, narrative, abstract
elaborations.movement principles: alignment (mobility, stability, plumbline), weight transfer, flexibility, strength, balance, coordination
elaborations.character: in drama, taking on and exploring the thoughts, perceptions, feelings, and beliefs of another
elaborations.metre: groupings or patterns of strong and weak beats
elaborations.duration: the length of a sound or silence in relation to the beat
elaborations.rhythm: the arrangement of sounds and silences over time
elaborations.tempo: the frequency or speed of the beat
elaborations.pitch: how high or low a note is
elaborations.texture: simultaneous layering of sounds (e.g., multi-part music making)
elaborations.notation: could include use of traditional and non-traditional notation (e.g., guitar tablature); in dance, this can include written formal and informal systems of symbols, shapes, and lines that represent body position and movement; in drama this can include diagrams indicating stage directions
elaborations.principles of design: the planned use of the visual elements to achieve a desired effect
elaborations.pattern: a design in which shapes, colours or lines repeat with regularity
elaborations.repetition: using the same object, colour, marking, or type of line more than once
elaborations.balance: a principle of design concerned with the arrangement of one or more of the elements so that they give a sense of equilibrium in design and proportion (e.g., radial, symmetrical, or asymmetrical) Content – Elaborations Grade 7
elaborations.movement: deliberate control of the viewer’s visual path across a work (e.g., a strong diagonal thrust of a colour)
elaborations.proportion: the relationship in size of parts, to a whole, and to one another
elaborations.unity, harmony: these concepts are closely related and often overlap; elements are used to create a sense of completeness

elaborations.technologies: includes both manual and digital technologies (e.g., electronic media, production elements, information technology, sound equipment and recording technologies, etc.); in visual arts, any visual image-making technology (e.g., paintbrush, scissors, pencil, stamp) and includes the improvisational use of miscellaneous items

elaborations.drama conventions: established ways of working in drama that explore meaning; drama techniques

Take musical risks to experience synchronicity among ensemble members and their audience

elaborations.purposeful play: learning that uses real-life and/or imaginary situations to engage and challenge learners’ thinking. Through planned purposeful play, students express their natural curiosity while exploring the world around them. It also provides a means for high-level reasoning and problem solving in a variety of ways.

content.text: dance elements, techniques, and vocabulary to create mood and convey ideas, including but not limited to: body, space, dynamics, time, relationships, form, and movement principles

elaborations.dance: the elements of dance are universally present in all dance forms and grow in sophistication over time
elaborations.body: what the body is doing, including whole or partial body action, types of movement (locomotor and non-locomotor), etc.
elaborations.space: where the body is moving, including place, level, direction, pathway, size/reach, shape, etc.
elaborations.time: how the body moves in relation to time, including beat (underlying pulse), tempo, and rhythmic patterns
elaborations.relationships: with whom or what the body is moving; movement happens in a variety of relationship including pairs, groups, objects, and environments
elaborations.form: The shape or structure of a dance; the orderly arrangement of thematic material. For example: phrase, beginning, middle, end, ABA, canon, call and response, narrative, abstract
elaborations.movement principles: include alignment (mobility, stability, plumbline), weight transfer, flexibility, strength, balance, and coordination
elaborations.dynamics: relative and changing levels of sound volume (e.g., forte, piano, decrescendo)
elaborations.movement: deliberate control of the viewer’s visual path across a work (e.g., a strong diagonal thrust of a colour)

content.text: choreographic devices and notation

elaborations.choreographic devices: strategies to develop movement, motifs, and phrases (e.g., changing size, levels/planes, body parts, dynamics, order, repetition, inversion, fragmentation, retrograde)
elaborations.notation: could include use of traditional and non-traditional notation (e.g., guitar tablature)

elaborations.dance: the elements of dance are universally present in all dance forms and grow in sophistication over time

elaborations.technologies: includes both manual and digital technologies (e.g., electronic media, production elements, information technology, sound equipment and recording technologies, etc.)

elaborations.drama forms: a medium for the expression of dramatic meaning (e.g., improvisation, tableau, role-play, mime, readers theatre, story theatre); may involve the integration of a variety of media and a combination of the arts
elaborations.drama conventions: established ways of working in drama that explore meaning; drama techniques

content.text: music elements, principles, techniques, vocabulary, notation, and symbols to define style and convey ideas, including but not limited to: beat/pulse, metre, duration, rhythm, tempo, pitch, timbre, dynamics, form, texture

elaborations.form: The shape or structure of a dance; the orderly arrangement of thematic material. For example: phrase, beginning, middle, end, ABA, canon, call and response, narrative, abstract
elaborations.notation: could include use of traditional and non-traditional notation (e.g., guitar tablature)
elaborations.metre: groupings or patterns of strong and weak beats
elaborations.duration: the length of a sound or silence in relation to the beat
elaborations.rhythm: the combination of pattern and movement to create a feeling of organized energy
elaborations.tempo: the frequency or speed of the beat
elaborations.pitch: how high or low a note is
elaborations.timbre: the characteristic quality of a sound independent of pitch and dynamics; tone colour
elaborations.dynamics: relative and changing levels of sound volume (e.g., forte, piano, decrescendo)
elaborations.texture: simultaneous layering of sounds (e.g., multi-part music making)

content.text: visual arts elements, principles, and image design strategies to create mood and convey ideas, including but not limited to: elements of design: line, shape, space, texture, colour, form, value principles of design: pattern, repetition, balance, contrast, emphasis, rhythm, movement, unity, variety, proportion, harmony image design strategies: elaboration, simplification, magnification, reversal, fragmentation, distortion

elaborations.space: where the body is moving, including place, level, direction, pathway, size/reach, shape, etc.
elaborations.form: The shape or structure of a dance; the orderly arrangement of thematic material. For example: phrase, beginning, middle, end, ABA, canon, call and response, narrative, abstract
elaborations.rhythm: the combination of pattern and movement to create a feeling of organized energy
elaborations.texture: simultaneous layering of sounds (e.g., multi-part music making)
elaborations.principles of design: the planned use of visual elements to achieve a desired effect
elaborations.balance: the arrangement of one or more elements to give a sense of equilibrium in design and proportion (e.g., radial, symmetrical, or asymmetrical)
elaborations.movement: deliberate control of the viewer’s visual path across a work (e.g., a strong diagonal thrust of a colour)
elaborations.unity: a sense of oneness created by the relationship among the elements (e.g., colours and lines that work together)
elaborations.proportion: the relationship in size of parts, to a whole, and to one another
elaborations.harmony: components of the visual image relate to, and complement each other

curricular_competencies.text: Engage in a period of research and empathetic observation in order to understand design opportunities

curricular_competencies.text: Choose a design opportunity

content.text: proper technique for fundamental movement skills, including non-locomotor, locomotor, and manipulative skills

elaborations.non-locomotor: movement skills performed “on the spot” without travelling across the floor or surface; could include: — balancing — bending — twisting — lifting
elaborations.locomotor: movement skills that incorporate travelling across the floor or surface; could include: — rolling — jumping — hopping — running — galloping
elaborations.manipulative: movement skills involving the control of objects, such as balls, primarily with the hands or feet; may also involve racquets or bats; skills could include: — bouncing — throwing — catching — kicking — striking

content.text: how to participate in different types of physical activities, including individual and dual activities, rhythmic activities, and games

elaborations.individual and dual activities: activities that can be done individually and/or with others; could include: — jumping rope — swimming — running — bicycling — Hula Hoop
elaborations.rhythmic activities: activities designed to move our bodies in rhythm; could include: — dance — gymnastics
elaborations.games: types of play activities that usually involve rules, challenges, and social interaction; could include: — tag — parachute activities — co-operative challenges — Simon says — team games — traditional Aboriginal games

content.text: relationships between food, hydration, and health

elaborations.relationships between food, hydration, a: — food gives us energy and helps us grow — food connects to self, family, history, culture, tradition, nature and community Content – Elaborations Kindergarten — water refreshes us and helps us grow, learn and play

elaborations.practices: — getting adequate sleep — participating in physical activity — making healthy eating choices — participating in relaxing activities — illness prevention through washing hands and proper hygiene

elaborations.appropriate: (e.g., consensual touch that feels welcome and safe – medical checkups, high-fives)
elaborations.inappropriate: (e.g., touch that hurts or makes us feel uncomfortable – being hit, touching of private parts)

content.text: differences between the health components of fitness

elaborations.health components of fitness: five different aspects that influence overall health and effect our ability to meet the physical demands of everyday living; include: — muscular strength — exerting maximal force to lift and/or move an object (e.g., completing one push-up) — muscular endurance — ability of the muscles to exert submaximal force over a prolonged period of time (e.g., doing more than one push-up) — cardiovascular endurance — ability of the heart and lungs to work together and provide blood and oxygen to the working muscles over a period of time (e.g., jogging) — flexibility — the ability of a joint and the surrounding muscles to move through a full range of motion (e.g., stretching) — body composition — the ratio of fat tissue to fat-free body tissue

content.text: training principles to enhance personal fitness levels, including the FITT principle

elaborations.fitt principle: a guideline to help develop and organize personal fitness goals based on: — Frequency — how many days per week — Intensity — how hard one exercises in the activity (e.g., percentage of maximum heart rate) — Type — the type of activity or exercise, focusing on the fitness goal (e.g., jogging for cardio endurance) — Time — how long the exercise session lasts

content.text: proper technique for fundamental movement skills, including non-locomotor, locomotor, and manipulative skills

elaborations.non-locomotor: movements performed “on the spot” without travelling across the floor or surface; could include: — balancing — bending — twisting — lifting
elaborations.locomotor: movement skills that incorporate travelling across the floor or surface; could include: — rolling — jumping — hopping — running — galloping
elaborations.manipulative: movement skills involving the control of objects, such as balls, primarily with the hands or feet; may also involve racquets or bats; could include: — bouncing — throwing — catching — kicking — striking

content.text: movement concepts and strategies

elaborations.movement concepts: include: — body awareness (e.g., parts of the body, weight transfer) — spatial awareness (e.g., general spacing, directions, pathways) — effort awareness (e.g., speed, force) — relationships to/with others and objects
elaborations.strategies: plans and/or ideas that will help a player or team successfully achieve a movement outcome or goal (e.g., moving into space away from an opponent to receive a pass)

content.text: ways to monitor and adjust physical exertion levels

elaborations.monitor and adjust physical exertion lev: could include: — using heart rate monitors — checking pulse — checking rate of perceived exertion (e.g., a five-point scale to self-assess physical exertion level)
elaborations.physical: how students’ bodies are growing and changing during puberty and adolescence (e.g., identifying changes to body and self-concept)

content.text: how to participate in different types of physical activities, including individual and dual activities, rhythmic activities, and games

elaborations.individual and dual activities: activities that can be done individually and/or with others; could include: — jumping rope — swimming — running — bicycling — Hula Hoop
elaborations.rhythmic activities: activities designed to move our bodies in rhythm; could include: — dancing Content – Elaborations Grade 7 — gymnastics
elaborations.games: types of play activities that usually involve rules, challenges, and social interaction; could include: — tag — parachute activities — co-operative challenges — Simon says — team games — traditional Aboriginal games
elaborations.physical: how students’ bodies are growing and changing during puberty and adolescence (e.g., identifying changes to body and self-concept)

content.text: training principles to enhance personal fitness levels, including the FITT principle, SAID principle, and specificity

elaborations.fitt principle: a guideline to help develop and organize personal fitness goals based on: — Frequency — how many days per week — Intensity — how hard one exercises in the activity (e.g., percentage of maximum heart rate) — Type — the type of activity or exercise, focusing on the fitness goal (e.g., jogging for cardio endurance) — Time — how long the exercise session lasts
elaborations.said principle: (Specific Adaptation to Imposed Demand): the body will react and respond to the type of demand placed on it (e.g., a student’s flexibility will eventually improve if he or she participates in regular stretching activities)
elaborations.specificity: the types of exercises chosen will determine the kinds of fitness improvements (e.g., a student who wants to improve his or her flexibility levels would participate in stretching exercises)

content.text: effects of different types of physical activity on the body

elaborations.effects: effects on the body produced by physical activities could include: — strengthening muscles and bones in activities where you have to move and/or control some type of weight (e.g., fitness circuits and/or jumping and landing) — strengthening heart and lungs in activities where you are moving at a fast pace (e.g., jogging or running) for periods of time (e.g., games, swimming, biking) — reducing stress and/or anxiety levels in activities where you can participate outside and/or elevate the heart rate
elaborations.physical: how students’ bodies are growing and changing during puberty and adolescence (e.g., identifying changes to body and self-concept)

content.text: factors that influence personal eating choices

elaborations.personal eating choices: influences could include — family traditions — personal taste and texture preferences — allergies — culture and celebrations — media messages — peers — differing options in various settings (e.g., school, home, community)

content.text: practices that reduce the risk of contracting sexually transmitted infections and life-threatening communicable diseases
sources of health information

elaborations.sources of health information: could include: — medical professionals — professionally produced health pamphlets — eHealth information — community support services

content.text: basic principles for responding to emergencies

elaborations.responding to emergencies: basic principles include: — following safety guidelines — having an emergency response plan — knowing how to get help

content.text: strategies to protect themselves and others from potential abuse, exploitation, and harm in a variety of settings

elaborations.strategies: plans and/or ideas that will help a player or team successfully achieve a movement outcome or goal (e.g., moving into space away from an opponent to receive a pass)

content.text: consequences of bullying, stereotyping, and discrimination
signs and symptoms of stress, anxiety, and depression

elaborations.signs and symptoms of stress, anxiety, a: could include: — problems sleeping — restlessness — loss of appetite and energy — wanting to be away from friends and/or family

content.text: influences of physical, emotional, and social changes on identities and relationships

elaborations.physical: how students’ bodies are growing and changing during puberty and adolescence (e.g., identifying changes to body and self-concept)
elaborations.emotional: how students’ thoughts and feelings might evolve or change during puberty and adolescence (e.g., managing impulses and intense feelings)
elaborations.social changes: how students interact with others and how their relationships might evolve or change during puberty and adolescence (e.g., recognizing that personal boundaries may change over time and ongoing consent is required; demonstrating situational awareness and responding to social cues – such as changes in mood)

content.text: French alphabet
French phonemes

elaborations.phonemes: individual speech sounds (for example, b, s, o, nasal vowels, r, u/ou); students are expected to be aware of and attempt to produce French phonemes, but they are not expected to master them

content.text: gender and number

elaborations.gender and number: introduction to masculine and feminine forms of words (gender); for example, the determiners le, la, un, une; singular and plural forms of words (number); for example, the determiners un, une versus des, and le, la versus les

content.text: common, high-frequency vocabulary and sentence structures for communicating meaning: asking and responding to simple questions expressing basic information about themselves and others expressing likes, dislikes, and preferences providing simple descriptions describing common elements of cultural festivals and celebrations

elaborations.simple questions: for example, Comment…?; Est-ce que…?; Où…?; Quand…?; Quel…?; Qu’est-ce que…?; Qui…?
elaborations.expressing basic information about thems: basic expressions used in greetings, salutations, and getting to know others; for example, Bonjour; Salut; Comment ça va?; Quel âge as-tu?; Je m’appelle…; J’ai…ans; Je suis…; J’aime…
elaborations.expressing likes, dislikes, and preferen: for example, J’aime…; J’adore…; Je n’aime pas…; Je déteste…; Je préfère…
elaborations.providing simple descriptions: using descriptive words, such as numbers, colours, sizes, and words for other physical attributes
elaborations.describing common elements of cultural f: for example, activities, clothing, dance, decorations, First Peoples regalia, food, music, parades, sports

content.text: location of Francophone and Francophone
a Francophone cultural festival or celebration in Canada

elaborations.a francophone cultural festival or celeb: for example, le Carnaval de Québec, le Festival Acadien de Caraquet, le Festival du Voyageur, le Festival du Bois, Métis Fest

curricular_competencies.text: Begin to recognize the relationship between French letter patterns and pronunciation

elaborations.begin to recognize the relationship betw: Students should begin to identify some groupings of letters that make the same sound (e.g., au, aux, eau, and ô), rhyming words, and letter patterns that have consistent pronunciations (e.g., ait, -ment, oi, –tion, and others)

Appreciate the importance of respect, inclusivity, and other positive behaviours in diverse, collaborative learning, and work environments
Question self and others about the reciprocal relationship between self and community

elaborations.reciprocal relationship: Individuals both support and draw support from communities.

curricular_competencies.text: Use entrepreneurial and innovative thinking to solve problems

elaborations.entrepreneurial: taking risks in order to create opportunities
elaborations.innovative: original, creative; taking an existing idea and making it better

curricular_competencies.text: Demonstrate leadership skills through collaborative activities in the school and community
Demonstrate safety skills in an experiential learning environment
Set realistic short- and longer-term learning goals, define a path, and monitor progress
Recognize the influence of peers, family, and communities on career choices and attitudes toward work

elaborations.attitudes toward work: Habits of mind and motivation are strongly influenced by models, both positive and negative.

curricular_competencies.text: Appreciate the value of new experiences, innovative thinking and risk taking in broadening their career options

elaborations.innovative: original, creative; taking an existing idea and making it better
elaborations.risk taking: exploring new activities to develop skills, interests, and passions, such as applied skills, arts, athletics, and academics

curricular_competencies.text: Explore volunteer opportunities and other new experiences outside school and recognize their value in career development
Apply project management skills to support career development

curricular_competencies.text: Use self-assessment and reflection to develop awareness of their strengths, preferences, and skills
Question self and others about how individual purposes and passions can support the needs of the local and global community when considering career choices
Recognize the impact of personal public identity in the world of work

curricular_competencies.text: Demonstrate respect, collaboration, and inclusivity in working with others to solve problems
Recognize and explore diverse perspectives on how work contributes to our community and society

elaborations.explore diverse perspectives: question and challenge career perceptions and possible career paths and analyze the relationships between work, society, and the economy

curricular_competencies.text: Demonstrate safety skills and appreciate the importance of workplace safety
Set and achieve realistic learning goals with perseverance and resilience
Recognize the influence of curriculum choices and co-curricular activities on career paths

elaborations.career paths: include direct to work, apprenticeships, college, or university

curricular_competencies.text: Appreciate the value of a network of resources and mentors to assist with career exploration

elaborations.mentors: include parents, teachers, elders, coaches, extended family, other adults, or peers

curricular_competencies.text: Question self and others about the role of family expectations and traditions, and of community needs in career choices
Apply a variety of research skills to expand their knowledge of diverse career possibilities and understand career clusters

elaborations.research skills: interviewing, investigating, exploring, experiencing, etc.; learning can come from memory, history, and story
elaborations.career clusters: a group of careers that share common skills and training
